step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Extend RIGHT x20 to push blocks')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 97, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: MOUSE on Blue block did nothing. Blocks stuck at cols 24-27. Need to try clicking on bottom panel blocks or find another mechanic.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Try MOUSE on bottom panel blocks to see if they trigger any action. Also try extending RIGHT further to see if blocks move more.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 96 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 4 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
MOUSE on the bottom panel didn't change anything either. The blocks are still stuck at cols 24-27.
I'm stuck in a loop. Let me reconsider the entire game from scratch.
The key issue is that Blue and Green are both at cols 24-27, and I can't release them. The retract mechanism isn't working.
Let me think about what might have gone wrong. Earlier, I successfully grabbed Green, moved the agent, and retracted to release Green. But now, after grabbing Blue and moving the agent, Blue got stuck. Then I grabbed Green, and Green also got stuck.
Maybe the issue is that I moved the agent AFTER grabbing Blue, which broke the connection. The retract only works if the agent is still aligned with the grabbed block.
Let me try a different approach: instead of trying to fix the current state, let me see if I can complete the level with the current arrangement.
The bottom panel shows Pink, Red, Green, Blue. The current arrangement is Red (13-16), Blue (19-22), Green (31-34). The target order is Red, Green, Blue.
But wait, maybe the bottom panel doesn't indicate the order of blocks. Maybe it indicates something else, like the colors that need to be collected or connected.
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is to have the blocks in the same order as the bottom panel, from left to right. But the blocks are arranged vertically, not horizontally. So maybe the goal is to have them in the same order from top to bottom.
Currently: Red, Blue, Green (top to bottom)
Target: Red, Green, Blue (top to bottom)
So I need to swap Blue and Green. But I can't because they're stuck.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to extend RIGHT even further to move the blocks off the board, which might reset them. Or maybe I need to move the agent to a specific position to trigger level completion.
Actually, let me try extending RIGHT many times to see if the blocks move further right and eventually get released or reset.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Stuck in loop. Blue and Green both at cols 24-27, can't release. MOUSE doesn't work on blocks or bottom panel.
Plan: Try extending RIGHT many times to push blocks further right - might trigger release or level completion.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try extending RIGHT many times to push blocks further
predict(expect_change=True, note='Extend RIGHT x20 to push blocks')
action(['RIGHT'] * 20)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After RIGHT x20 ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"  Agent: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== After RIGHT x20 ===
  R: rows 13-16, cols 18-21
  b: rows 19-22, cols 24-27
  N: rows 31-34, cols 42-45
  Agent: rows 30-35
Board changed: True
Level completed: False
